speaker: interviewer
text: --tape four of the interview with Aplonia Koedam on November 21, 1996. How many members of your family would you say were in the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two brothers and [? us then. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- Simon was one brother. And who was the other--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Jo, Johannes--

speaker: interviewer
text: And Johannes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was doing-- mainly, when he started out with the group, getting cards, ration cards. And it was then the ration cards were mainly, of course, for the one who were hiding. Whatever it was, hiding underground and the Jews, or people who didn't go to work and will-- they didn't want to go to Germany and couldn't find any other place, they were hiding, too. And they all needed food. And they had no [? aid of-- ?] no ration cards. So they went after those offices where they were given out the ration cards. And it was done on a monthly basis, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were active, you know, in robbing cards at those distribution centers. And that was not easy, of course. But they did it from one place to the other. They were-- could never find them. They tried to find them. But they went from left to right, north to south. You know? They-- they were very sought after by the [GERMAN], the Germans. It was a very harsh, harsh-- how do you call this section, whatever. And if they caught him, there was-- Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: --no way out anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kind of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less, the Gestapo, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Was your brother caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost. Almost, he was caught. He was-- at that time, he was a commander. And he was also involved in that-- the weapon dropping, the weapon dropping. And the-- the weapons them self, of course, had to be hidden too. And they were in haystacks or whatever. But-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but once it happened, that they hit and there was eight bombs of last drop. It was close to the end of the war. I think it was in October or something, or beginning of November. They had the last drop. And everybody who worked in, they went-- it was always during the middle of the night, on a moonlit night. They went to each their hiding places. And one group went to a hiding place where they were supposed to go. And they did. And there was also, at the same time, there was a news, like, something like that, news people to try to get the illegal news out that was on that same address. And somehow, the Germans sniffed it out, that there was a station where they got the news. And they went after it. The same moment that the group came back from the weapon dropping and hiding the weapons, they came in the same house. And they were the Germans. So they had a really-- they had a good [? hunt. ?] And of course, they were beaten in the first place. And then they were arrested. And it was my brother's group. There were about, oh, 10 or 11. And a few went to their own hiding places before. So they were all arrested. And after that, the group was just split. There was nothing left. And it took a few weeks. And they did not feel happy about it. And they knew they were-- they were-- were tortured, terrible. But it seemed to be that nobody gave in, seems to be. And then the group was left, a few came in to help out, new ones. And my brother and a few other ones got a plan to get them out of the jail. They were-- so far that they knew, after all the interrogations they would be sentenced to death. It was getting closer and closer. And they made a plan. They made a plan, figured it all out from the night, from the minute, from the date. And finally, it was set, I think, for December the 10th. And then there was a group who were then really the leader of the trucks where they would get the prisoners in if that ever, you know, worked out. And it was the leader of the group who had to get them out. And to make a story short, it was very, very, very dangerous. They waited for the shifting of the Germans. Through the days-- during the day, I think they were just [? about, ?] [? whatever. ?] But it was a time of shifting that they opened the door. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was still dark in the morning. And everybody was alert. And there was one girl who worked in there, too. And they-- they were standing alert, you know, when they are coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had, then, a way of telling them that the group was on their way, that with a truck for the people so if they come out, they were on their way. At that minute-- minute-- limit of time, they had to get these people out. And they had it all figured out when the change of the one group, the other. What was really dumb from the Germans, whatever, there was a time limit when there was almost nobody in, only the doormen. And that time was about, I think, 20 minutes to get them out. And when that time was there, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brother was down standing in the corner. And the other one was over there at the side of that jail. So if anything broke out, that he could get him out of the fire or whatever, what's going to happen. So one knocked on the door. And the door went open. And my brother-- psh-- went in and put his foot through the door. And he said, you have to open up. And the man said, no, I won't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he said, would you like that? And he clicked his revolver. And he let him go.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The man was hiding behind the door. And he said, yeah, you have to open the cell doors. In the meantime, number three came, number four came, the whole group, to get those people out. They took the phone off the hook. And they opened the doors. And you can imagine what they went through. They couldn't believe. They couldn't that was going to happen. They thought they  were picked up to get to the execution place. And then they saw you know, the group, their friends. They were the friends who were taken, you know, from that place where they-- the Germans found out where they were. Those were the same people that were in jail. Well, anyway, he got them all out. And they got them out. And they knew they would be beaten, not hard. But it was all-- they were dressed in German uniforms, also. They had, all, German uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: So even people who even saw it happen, they thought it was just people were taken out to wherever. And they spoke German. And they said [SPEAKING GERMAN], and all that stuff, you know, and [GERMAN], and everything. And they beat them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even people were standing, you know, they were yelling at them. Well, it was-- in the meantime, it was light outside. I think there is more to the story if you go in all details. But the main thing, that he got them all out, and just a few days before they were executed. And they hid again. Because that was a big thing after that. It had happened in Assen, in Holland. That is a town in northern province. And after it happened, he heard there was a-- a-- a terrible rush of cars and trucks, jeeps, all-- whatever there was-- over to find the prisoners and the ones who rescued them. None of them was found. But I think two, after that, were shot. Two of those prisoners after that were shot. They found them in their hiding place, somehow. And that is quite a story, too--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --he did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's incredible. What do you remember, the days leading up to the liberation and Liberation Day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember very well. We were so hungry. Because that part of the Netherlands, they never came to-- that-- one part of the Netherlands-- Netherlands was liberated. After that happened, I think they were liberated in September, before May. That was September 5th, '44. And then a big part of the Netherlands was liberated. They were so close for about, oh-- oh-- oh, 40 miles from us, that we thought they were coming. And there was even a day that they were so close that we went to the highway-- our street crossed the highway-- to greet them. Because they were so close, that we thought the tanks and everything was coming. Well, because they were going to Rotterdam. And it was just past our street. And the whole town was waiting and waiting over there to greet them. And we were liberated. That was in September. That was it. That was-- I think it was the 5th of September. And we stood there the whole day. And nobody came. Nobody came. They didn't liberate us. And-- and that we were never really liberated until the war was over. And it was already the western part what wasn't liberated. And that was where we lived. And that where is the most population was. And there was-- was the hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was the-- the-- the-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --famine, right? That was what it was. And it getting getting worse and worse and worse. And people died. And-- and that was-- oh, we were so hungry. Cor got sick. He got hunger [? edema, ?] that the legs get swollen and tired and-- and then there was another thing, what he don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then all of a sudden, the door-- knocked on the door. And there is food for your husband. So they knew that. Because he went to the doctor. And I think the doctor was involved, again, to get an organization starting for people who were very sick because of the hunger. And that, they didn't say anything. Only this is for your husband. They didn't say what, where from them. They left, no word, no questions asked. That is how it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about for your little children? You-- how did you  get food for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was very hard. The milk-- our oldest was two years old. He couldn't get any milk anymore. And the milk was skimmed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my younger son did not grow well at all. And the doctor said, he is-- he-- he is healthy. Don't be afraid. He will make it. And-- and-- and he was always laughing. And he wasn't-- he was a trooper, as long as-- as little as he was. When the war was over, did you ever get in contact with the family that you had hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: That took-- yeah. That took a little while. Because the mail did not work either, nothing. And the trains were not-- the-- the-- the trains did not ride. And-- [SIGHS] --there was almost no transportation. We had no telephone. It was about three months, I think, after the war finally, that we found out that they made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't remember. Or they-- maybe they-- they-- they-- they sent us a letter or something. But they came to visit us, of course. And that was just-- oh, we couldn't believe it. We didn't say anything. But our eyes, we look at our eyes, we made it. You know? And Dolfi was four years old already. And she remembered, of course, Harro. That was her baby-- baby brother. And she couldn't believe he was walking and everything. He was so cute. And only until-- in the meantime, we had our new baby. And he was adored. And we kept a long time, we kept up the friendship until yeah, they died a natural death.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Dolfi is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Dolfi is-- lives in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the last time we were in Holland was last year. We did not see her. We were there only a short time. But mostly when we were in Holland, we would go to visit her. Or she visited us wherever we were. And we always said, there's always a room for you, you are here. Here is your bedroom, you know, in my house. And yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Dolfi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name? Is she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But she got divorced. She has her own name again, Konyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has she got children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two. Robin and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I think I have it here. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how-- how-- how old her children are now? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they are maybe about 19 or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right, if--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I suppose your family has asked you why-- why did you put yourself in danger to-- to help others? And do you have any thoughts as to why you did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We couldn't stand what was happening. We just opened our house to hope everything will be all right. And we survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We took chances, we know that. But I think when you're young, I think you take easier chances, maybe when you're older. And not only that, but the whole situation was just-- it was war. So many things were done, what you never do before. We stole. We didn't steal from the-- from the-- from the stores. But we-- but I said, we stole a sign. That was stealing, really. That was from the Germans. And we burned it in our-- we had no fuel. So we think it was a good course. Because they lost the directions and we-- we were warm. I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a road sign that--

speaker: interviewee
text: A road sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A road sign, where-- where to go. It was big. So we had a good warm place. We burned it in our fireplace, those things. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And once they came to pick up men, too, on our street. And we got-- we heard it. And didn't know what to do at the moment. And they say, ooh, we have to go. We have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, where? Now, we were-- we had a meadow in the back. And I said, the best thing is to go in the meadow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a boy next door. He was only 18. And he want to go away too. So quickly, quickly. It was-- oh, it was still light outside. And then it was getting dark. So they took a sheet with them. And it's snowy. It was snow. So they walked from over a brook into the meadow. And they put the sheets over their head, that they were not seen, because the snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were sitting, all laying in the snow with sheets over their heads. And they passed the house. There was nobody there. After the war, you had more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Two daughters--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Lannika ?] and [? Helme, ?] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what brought you to-- why did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought at that time, Europe was so-- really so upside down with everything. And it was very hard to get a good job. And I had a brother living here. And he said, oh, there is so much work for you. Because Cor was into building. And maybe it is better, you know, that there is so much opportunity here in the United States. And maybe it is better that we go with the boys, especially also, for the boys, to get maybe, a better future than it was in Europe. Plus, the fact that in the Netherlands themselves, and maybe in other countries, they really tried to get people to emigrate too. Because there was not enough housing. Because so much was bombed. There was not enough housing and not enough jobs yet-- jobs yet, you know, after the war. There was, of course, cleaning to do and rebuilding. That was plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: But all the other companies were not working yet. You know? Other companies, whatever, your auto industry, or whatever, or-- or furniture industry. There was-- it was so radical destroyed in so many countries, that I think it is the almost the biggest immigration time in the later history.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] I think that time, after the war, Canada, Australia, they went. People went to Australia, to Canada, the United States, were the three main immigration countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your own family in Germany, your siblings, mostly, except for the brother here, did-- did most of them stay in-- in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a-- two brothers here. The first brother, what I talk about, he came later. And a sister here. And she married an American that she met him in-- in-- in Arabia, when she was a nurse over there. And she met him over  there. So she married an American. So she came to live in America. And one brother, he was in America when the war broke out. So we didn't hear from him. And it was long after the war until we heard he was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he worked here-- he went here in the Navy. Yeah, could it be the Navy? Or the Marines. And he had quite some story-- had quite some stories to tell, too, where they were torpedoed several times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were all up in the pole, North Pole, over there. When that was also in Russia and Finland. And he had a very hard time too. But he served here, during the war, in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family-- were they very surprised when they found--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what you had done during-- you know, to-- to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --know that you had been hiding a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from-- from-- from-- from my Simon, they knew, of course, when I was picked up. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but your own--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. They're surprised. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you kept-- did you keep letters or correspondence with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Konyn family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Christmas and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: --between. And when we were still in Holland, they came for birthdays. And they were crazy about Harro, because Dolfi, always played-- well, they were six months with us. And they loved him. you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they-- do-- do they have any contact, Dolfi and Harro, as they grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sometimes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they have a friendship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it's--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have pictures. When they see pictures, she's excited. Oh, there's Harro. And And he heard she had a computer. And he would like to pick it up, you know, with the computer, to-- it's whatever, or what is all the possibilities nowadays, on those computers. And we-- we just were talking about it last week with  him. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, to end the tape, if there is anything that you would like to-- to say about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say of our experience that we-- that I never was sorry I did it. And so was Cor. We were never sorry. And we were so glad that we did it. Because at least those people survived. And I'm sure a lot of other people did the same thing like us. And they survived. I know there were not many people in the Netherlands anymore. I know that. But the ones who were hidden, a lot of them survived. And a lot of them did not, like the people, my brother. They didn't survive. We have never heard of them anymore. They are murdered. And [INAUDIBLE] are murdered. His daughter is murdered. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sad time. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are welcome. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] These are my parents at their wedding day. I remember my mom telling us she made the dress all herself. And if you can see it closely, she really made a dress. It's beautiful. Now, I think I wish I could have worn it. But I had my own bridal gown.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Cornelis. And it was Bastiaanse. That's my maiden name then. They were-- And my mother's maiden name was Aplonia, like my name. And her last name was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what year this would have been, when they got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where they got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they got married in Rotterdam, also. Yes, yeah, I am sure. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is me, that little girl there on the right side. At that time, I was five years old. And the little boy was my brother, who was six years old. And unfortunately, he died shortly after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Lennard. And we were-- when we were small, we were very close. And I remember little things. He was so protective of me. We had a dog next door, neighbor. And I was afraid. And when we came home from school, he would walk the side of the dog, that I wasn't close to the dog. And I was kind of shy too, when I went to the kindergarten. And there was always a girl when I went, and they would sit in my seat. She always took the seat. And then I walk around and she took the seat again. He knew that. And then he went with me in the classroom and make sure that I had my seat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an angel. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is when I am like, knitting a knot on a large, large, rug. That was a wedding gift from the women of Rotterdam, as a gift for the Queen Julianus.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Which year? That was done in-- 18, I think it was-- was about '38, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are some bridal pictures. We got married on September 13, 1939. And you can see here, where we just come out of church on the left-hand side. On the right-hand side we are, I think, walking into the church. On the left bottom are my parents. And the right bottom--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] I think we're just focusing-- are we just focusing on-- the whole page. All right, yes, that's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right bottom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. In the right bottom, you see two grandmothers, there are Cor's grandmothers and his sister in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and your parents are on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left, Bob. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, as you can see, our formal wedding picture. And it was a great day. We will never forget. And what is right underneath is a announcement of our--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's-- it's not on camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just the photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I thought he said the upper half.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, just the upper picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK. This is a picture that was the last picture, what was taken before we left for the United States. As you can see from the left to right, the left is my husband, Cornelis. And then comes our son, Pim, and then myself. And then my son, Harro. And to the left again, that is our little daughter, [? Helme, ?] at the time, and our daughter, Aplonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aplonia's the on in the-- in the lower left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from the family we were hiding during the war. As you can see from the top, that is [? Max ?] Konyn. And then his wife, [? Mart ?] Konyn. And then his little daughter in dark clothes is Dolfi Konyn. She is-- was 2 and 1/2 years old when they came to live with us for that time. Then what is on [? Max's ?] left is [? our ?] little son, [INAUDIBLE] and Harro. And all the way on the left bottom is his cousin, [? Hans. ?]
